aximar.title: Complex Numbers
aximar.description: Arithmetic, polar form, De Moivre, roots of unity, complex calculus, and plotting.

cells.source:
# Complex Numbers in Maxima

A tour of complex number operations: arithmetic, polar and exponential forms, De Moivre's theorem, roots of unity, complex calculus, and visualisation.
## Basic Arithmetic
## Real Part, Imaginary Part, Modulus, Argument

z : 3 + 4*%i;
[realpart(z), imagpart(z), cabs(z), carg(z)]
## Conjugate

z : 3 + 4*%i;
[conjugate(z), rectform(z * conjugate(z))]

## Polar Form & Euler's Formula

`polarform(z)` converts to r·e^(iθ). Maxima knows Euler's identity e^(iπ) + 1 = 0.
polarform(3 + 4*%i)

/* Euler's identity */
rectform(%e^(%i * π) + 1)

## De Moivre's Theorem

(cos θ + i sin θ)ⁿ = cos(nθ) + i sin(nθ). Use `demoivre` to convert exponential form to trig form.

## Roots of Unity

The nth roots of unity are the solutions to zⁿ = 1.

/* 5th roots of unity via explicit formula */
makelist(rectform(%e^(2*π*%i*k/5)), k, 0, 4)

## Complex Functions & Calculus

Differentiation and integration work with complex-valued expressions.

/* Derivative of a complex exponential */
diff(%e^(%i*ω*t), t)

/* Integral of e^(iωt) */
integrate(%e^(%i*ω*t), t)

## Expanding Complex Trig & Hyperbolic Functions

`exponentialize` converts trig to exponentials; `demoivre` converts back.

/* cos(z) in terms of exponentials */
exponentialize(cos(z))

/* Verify: cos(iz) = cosh(z) */
rectform(exponentialize(cos(%i*z) - cosh(z)))
## Solving Equations with Complex Roots

/* Quadratic with complex roots */
solve(x^2 + 2*x + 5 = 0, x)

/* Cubic: all roots including complex ones */
solve(x^3 - 1 = 0, x)

## Complex Logarithm & Powers

The complex logarithm is multi-valued. Maxima returns the principal branch.

/* Principal log of a negative number */
rectform(log(-1))

/* i^i is real! */
rectform(%i^%i)

/* Its numerical value */
float(%)

## Complex Series Expansion

Taylor-expand complex functions around a point.

/* Taylor series of e^(iz) around z=0 */
taylor(%e^(%i*z), z, 0, 6)

## Mapping a Circle under z²

When we square every point on a circle in the complex plane, the image is a distorted curve. Here we plot a circle of radius 1 centred at ½ and its image under f(z) = z².

/* Circle: z = 1/2 + e^(iθ)   Image: w = z² */
a : 1/2$
plot2d(
  [[parametric, a + cos(t), sin(t), [t, 0, 2*float(%pi)]],
   [parametric, (a+cos(t))^2 - sin(t)^2, 2*(a+cos(t))*sin(t), [t, 0, 2*float(%pi)]]],
  [xlabel, "Re"], [ylabel, "Im"],
  [legend, "z", "z^2"],
  [same_xy])
